coredata.dc:title: Intrinsic dual emissive carbon dots for ratiometric sensing of acetylcholinesterase fluctuation induced by organophosphorus pesticide intoxication
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Haimin
Zhang, Qianyu
Luo, Xueting
Yao, Zhihao
Qu, Yaqin
Wang, Erjing
Jiang, Bingbing
Qiu, Zhenpeng
Li, Cao
Xu, Ziqiang
coredata.dc:description: Recently, the ratiometric analysis of AChE activity based on the intrinsic dual-emissive fluorescent (FL) nanomaterials is urgently required. The AChE activity can serve as a promising biomarker of neuro-toxicity induced by organophosphorus pesticides. In this work, oxidized carbon dots (ox-CDs) with intrinsic dual FL peaks at 650 and 680 nm are obtained. Moreover, the FL peak at 680 nm can be effectively quenched by Ag+ or Hg2+ while that at 650 nm can be served as self-reference signal. The introduction of AChE and acetylthiocholine (ATCh) contribute to the generation of TCh, which can capture the metal ions via competitive association to recover their FL signal to the initial status. Furthermore, the AChE activity can be determined by the ratio of FL intensity at 680 nm to that at 650 nm. As for the ox-CDs-Ag+ system, the linear range is determined to be 0.5–10 mU/mL and LOD is 0.127 mU/mL, while those for ox-CDs-Hg2+ system are 0.05–1.6 mU/mL and 0.019 mU/mL, respectively. Furthermore, the fluctuation of plasma and cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) AChE activity in dichlorvos-poisoned and pralidoxime therapeutic rats has been monitored by the ox-CDs-Ag+ system, which is essential for early diagnostics and prognostics of dichlorvos triggered neurotoxicity.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Acetylcholinesterase
Ratiometric fluorescent
Organophosphorus intoxication

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: AChE activity detection with ratiometric fluorescence
Molecular mechanism of interaction between CDs and metal ions
Detection of AChE activity as a biomarker for OPs poisoning

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We gratefully acknowledge financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 22073025 , 21603067 , 52073082 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of CDs (A) UV–vis absorption spectra and FL spectra of different CDs；(B) The FL decay curves of Raw CDs and ox-CDs excited by 375 nm with emission at 680 nm. (C) The HRTEM image of ox-CDs. Scale Bar: 20 nm. Inset: size distribution of ox-CDs. (D) The XRD patterns of different CDs, (E) The XPS survey spectrum of different CDs. (F) The FTIR spectra of raw CDs and ox-CDs.

. (A) Several control experiments to verify the feasibility and mechanism of detecting AChE activity by the ox-CDs-Ag+ system. (B) The FL spectra of the ox-CDs-Ag+ system after incubation with varied amounts of AChE; (C) Plot of the ratio of F 680/F 650 versus AChE concentration of the ox-CDs-Ag+ system. (D) Control experiments to confirm the important role of sulfdryl in detection of AChE activity of the ox-CDs-Ag+ system. The UV–vis absorption spectra (E) and the FL decay curves (F) of ox-CDs, ox-CDs incubated with Ag+ and ox-CDs-Ag+ system in the presence of TCh.
. (A) The plot of F 0/F relative the concentration of Ag+. (B) The plots of K app relative to the concentration of Ag+. The red line represents the fitting results according to Eq. (1).

The optimization of the detection conditions and linear relationship of the ox-CDs-Ag+ system. (A) The relationship between (F-F 0)/F 0 at 680 nm and concentration of ATCh. (B) The relationship between (F-F 0)/F 0 at 680 nm and aqueous pH. (C) The FL intensity at 680 nm relative to the reaction time in the presence of AChE incubated ATCh (1 mM). (D) The FL spectra of the ox-CDs-Ag+ with the addition of various concentration of AChE incubated with ATCh (1 mM). (E) The Plots of F 680/F 650 versus the concentration of AChE. (F) The linear relationship between the concentration of AChE and F 680/F 650. Error bars indicate standard deviations (n = 3).

(A) The FL spectra of the ox-CDs-Ag+ system in the presence of ATCh (1 mmol/L)+Plasma (Normal), ATCh (1 mmol/L)+Plasma (Dichlorvos), ATCh(1 mmol/L)+Plasma (PAM-1), ATCh (1 mmol/L)+Plasma (PAM-2); (B) The ratio of F 680/F 650 value of ox-CDs as displayed in Figure 6 A. (C) The FL spectra of the ox-CDs-Ag+ system in the presence of ATCh (1 mmol/L)+CSF (Normal), ATCh (1 mmol/L)+CSF (Dichlorvos), ATCh (1 mmol/L)+CSF (PAM-1), ATCh (1 mM)+CSF (PAM-2). (D) The F 680/F 650 value of ox-CDs as displayed in Fig. 6C. *P < 0.05, * *P < 0.01 and * **P < 0.001 versus the normal group as control.
The scheme for the detection of AChE activity by the changes of FL signal of dual emissive ox-CDs and metal ions.

head.ce:title: Intrinsic dual emissive carbon dots for ratiometric sensing of acetylcholinesterase fluctuation induced by organophosphorus pesticide intoxication

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Pharmacy, Hubei University of Chinese Medicine, Wuhan 430065, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Pharmacy, Hubei University of Chinese Medicine

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These two authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Recently, the ratiometric analysis of AChE activity based on the intrinsic dual-emissive fluorescent (FL) nanomaterials is urgently required. The AChE activity can serve as a promising biomarker of neuro-toxicity induced by organophosphorus pesticides. In this work, oxidized carbon dots (ox-CDs) with intrinsic dual FL peaks at 650 and 680 nm are obtained. Moreover, the FL peak at 680 nm can be effectively quenched by Ag+ or Hg2+ while that at 650 nm can be served as self-reference signal. The introduction of AChE and acetylthiocholine (ATCh) contribute to the generation of TCh, which can capture the metal ions via competitive association to recover their FL signal to the initial status. Furthermore, the AChE activity can be determined by the ratio of FL intensity at 680 nm to that at 650 nm. As for the ox-CDs-Ag+ system, the linear range is determined to be 0.5–10 mU/mL and LOD is 0.127 mU/mL, while those for ox-CDs-Hg2+ system are 0.05–1.6 mU/mL and 0.019 mU/mL, respectively. Furthermore, the fluctuation of plasma and cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) AChE activity in dichlorvos-poisoned and pralidoxime therapeutic rats has been monitored by the ox-CDs-Ag+ system, which is essential for early diagnostics and prognostics of dichlorvos triggered neurotoxicity.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Nowadays, the widespread application of organophosphorus pesticides (OPs) may contaminate the environment, which results in adverse effect on the health of human beings, especially the nervous system [1]. The main acute neurotoxicity of OPs is reflected in inhibiting the activity of acetylcholinesterase (AChE), which is a typical hydrolase in the cholinergic neurons [2]. Therefore, the AChE activity in cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) and peripheral blood can be identified as a biomarker for early diagnostics and prognostics of OPs triggered neurotoxicity, which is beneficial for the early diagnosis of acute OPs exposure-related dysfunctions.

Recently, several techniques have been developed to detect AChE activity, such as colorimetric assay, fluorescence (FL) techniques, high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC), gas chromatography (GC) and so on [3]. Among these strategies, FL techniques are particularly desirable, due to their high sensitivity, simplicity and short response time [4]. Recently, various materials have been applied to construct FL probes, which includes organic dyes [5], quantum dots [6], metal nanoclusters [7], rare earth-based materials [8] and carbon dots (CDs) [9]. Among these materials, the CDs hold distinct advantages, featured as adjustable emissions and superior biocompatibility, which emerged as promising candidates for real-time tracking of drugs and tumor therapy, especially medical diagnosis [10–13]. Currently, the CDs based fluorescent probe largely rely on several conventional FL sensing mechanism, such as inner filter effect, photoinduced electron transfer, and Forster resonance energy transfer [14,15].

In general, the low sensitivity of the CDs-based FL AChE sensors with a single FL peak is required to be improved [16,17]. Although the ratiometric biosensor can effectively improve the sensitivity and signal-to-noise ratio of the detection nanoplatform [18,19], an additional FL substances or nanomaterials (such as OPD, Tb/GMP ICP nanoparticles, 2,3-diaminophenazine, alizarin red S (ARS), CdTe QDs) [6,20–23], serve as a self-calibration signal, are required to fabricate the traditional CDs-based ratiometric FL AChE sensor, which may complicate the procedures of fabrication and detection, or even affect the stability of the system. Therefore, the ratiometric analysis of AChE activity based on the intrinsic FL features of ox-CDs has rarely been reported, which may contribute to simpler procedures and higher stability of the sensors.

Herein, the development of ratiometric-based AChE sensors based on intrinsic dual emissive CDs is urgently required. The ratiometric CDs-based AChE probe has been fabricated by combining intrinsic dual emissive CDs and metal ions (Ag+ or Hg2+) with higher affinity towards TCh. Firstly, red emissive CDs were synthesized according to our previous work [24]. Then the CDs were oxidized to realize dual emissions (λ em=650 and 680 nm), denoted as ox-CDs. The FL intensity of ox-CDs at 680 nm (F 680) decreased obviously while that at 650 nm (F 650) decreased slightly in the presence of metal ions. Moreover, the F 680 recovers obviously by the introduction of AChE and ATCh while F 650 nearly keeps constant which provides direct principles for the detection of AChE activity with ratiometric mode. Furthermore, this sensing strategy could be expanded to in vitro access to the OPs-induced acute neurotoxicity with the AChE activity as a biomarker.

Firstly, red emissive CDs were synthesized by solvothermal treatment of GSH solution in formamide according to previous work [24]. Then the CDs were oxidized by diluted HNO3 (0.125 mol/L) to obtain ox-CDs. In brief, HNO3 (0.125 mol/L) and CDs (10 mg) were mixed in 20 mL of deionized water. Then the mixture was refluxed for 24 h with magnetic stirring. After the reaction cooled naturally, the pH was adjusted to neutral with NaOH, and then it was filtered with a 0.22 µm microporous membrane. After filtration, the dialysis against water was performed (MW=1.0 kDa) for 24 h. Finally, the product is lyophilized to obtain the final products.

ce:section.ce:section-title: AChE activity detection with ratiometric fluorescence
ce:section.ce:para: The typical procedures for detection of AChE activity by the ox-CDs-Ag+ system was described as a model. The AChE and ATCh were mixed in Tris-HAc solution (10 mmol/L, pH 7.4) and incubated at 37 ºC for 30 min. Then above solution (200 μL) was diluted by the solution containing ox-CDs (4 μL, 5 mg/mL), AgNO3 (60 μL, 0.5 mmol/L) and Tris-HAc (1736 μL, 10 mmol/L, pH=7.0). After 1 min incubation, the FL spectra were recorded by the FL spectrophotometer with an excitation wavelength of 420 nm. The final concentration of ATCh was set as 1 mmol/L. The detection of AChE activity by the ox-CDs-Hg2+ system is similar to that of the ox-CDs-Ag+, except for the final concentration of Hg2+ and ATCh was set as 5 μmol/Land 0.1 mmol/L, respectively.
The limit of detection (LOD) for AChE is obtained by the following equation: LOD= 3σ/k, where σ is the standard deviation of the noise and k is the slope of the calibration plot.

Firstly, the ox-CDs were obtained by solvothermal treatment of GSH in formamide and subsequent oxidation. The FL spectra of ox-CDs show two prominent peaks centered at 680 and 650 nm ( Fig. 1A). The absorption spectra of ox-CDs are similar to that of raw CDs, while the shoulder peak of raw CDs at around 400 nm become more intense after oxidation (Fig. 1A), implying the content of oxygen-related groups increases obviously. The decay curves of ox-CDs can be fitted with the second exponential decal model, and the average FL lifetime was determined to be 4.90 ns (τ1 =4.77 ns, τ2 =8.81 ns) which is quite close to that of raw CDs (Fig. 1B). Due to bright FL signal in the near-infrared region, the FL of ox-CDs may be stemmed from the surface defects [25].

As shown in the TEM images of ox-CDs (Fig. 1C), a large amount of nearly mono-disperse quasi-spherical particles are observed and the statistical size is determined to be 2.3 ± 1.50 nm (inset of Fig. 1C). Compared with the size of raw CDs reported in our previous work (around 4 nm) [26], the size of ox-CDs decreases obviously, which may imply the CDs are etched by HNO3 during the oxidation process. Both raw CDs and ox-CDs show a single diffraction peak at about 26°, which confirms the presence of graphite structure (Fig. 1D). It is evident that the XPS spectra of raw CDs and ox-CDs show several peaks at 284.8, 400.0, 532.0 and 163.0 eV, implying the presence of C, N, O and S (Fig. 1E). Moreover, the successful oxidation of CDs can be confirmed by the relative content of each surface element (Table S1), namely the atomic ratio of O increases from 18.6 % to 22.2 %. On other hand, the deconvoluted N1s and O1s XPS spectra implies that the content of CN increases obviously while that of the hydroxyl also increase obviously (Fig. S1). Compared with the raw CDs, the width of the band of νO-H/N-H at about 3400 cm−1 broadens, which makes the absorption band between 3000 and 3200 cm−1 derived from the unsaturated carbon-hydrogen bond more intense [27] (Fig. 1F). Moreover, the peaks at around 1600 cm−1 derived from the skeletal vibrations of the aromatic ring increase obviously. Both trends confirmed the increase of CC and CN content. Moreover, the band of νC-O-C at 1047 cm−1 of raw CDs disappears in ox-CDs, attributing to the ether bond is oxidized to hydroxyl. This can be confirmed by the increasing of νC-O at about 1400 cm−1.

We have previously attributed the shift of FL peak from 680 to 650 nm of raw CDs under alkaline conditions to the (de)protonation and tautomerism between the structures of -NH-CO and -NC-OH [26]. We have found that the surface oxidation degrees and CC/CN increased obviously. After the -NH-CO is converted to -NC-OH, the CN content increase obviously. Moreover, more oxygen-related groups were formed by the oxidation of ether. Both factors contributed to the occurrence of a new peak at around 650 nm.

It is widely accepted that the oxygen-related groups can be considered as the good ligands for heavy metal ions [28], which results in the dramatic quenching of the FL signal. The AChE promotes the hydrolysis of acetylthiocholine (ATCh) to form TCh with sulfhydryl (-SH) with a higher affinity toward metal ions. Herein, the activity of AChE can be indirectly monitored by the changes in the FL signal of dual emissive ox-CDs ( Scheme 1). We have investigated the influence of various metal ions. It is pretty evident that several metal ions quench the FL intensity of ox-CDs obviously. However, the recovery of FL intensity can only be observed by incubating the ox-CDs-Ag+ or ox-CDs-Hg2+ system with AChE and ATCh (Fig. S2). With the introduction of metal ions (Ag+ or Hg2+), the F 680 decrease obviously while F 650 decrease slightly ( Figs. 2A and S3A). As expected, further addition of both ATCh and AChE contributes to the apparent recovery of F 680 and F 650 increases a little, which is achieved by the fact that TCh would capture metal ions from ox-CDs. Moreover, this competitive binding would finally recover ox-CDs to their initial status. With further increase AChE, the F 680 gradually increases while the F 650 almost keeps constant (Figs. 2B–C and S3B–C). It should be noted that the ratio of F 680 to F 650 (F 680/F 650) positively correlates with the activity of AChE within a certain range, so the assessment of the activity of AChE can be realized (Figs. 2C and S3C).

Then several control experiments were conducted to verify the mechanism. The introduction of mere ATCh or AChE to the metal ions quenched ox-CDs aqueous solution does not result in any changes in the FL spectra, indicating the key role of -SH in the recovery of the F 680 (Figs. 2A and S3A). After the F 680 of ox-CDs was quenched by metal ions, the addition of AChE and acetylcholine (ACh), in which the hydrolysis product is choline (Ch) without -SH, do not show any influence on the FL spectra of ox-CDs mixed with metal ions, which further confirm the critical role of -SH (Figs. 2D and S3D).

As shown in Fig. 2E, the absorption spectrum of ox-CDs changes obviously after the coordination with Ag+, featuring as the disappearance of the shoulder peak at 398 nm and merging of dual peaks to signal peak, which indicates the strong interaction between Ag+ and ox-CDs. As expected, with the introduction of AChE and ATCh, the absorption spectra of ox-CDs recover to their initial status. Similar reversible changes have also been observed for the Hg2+-based AChE detection strategy (Fig. S3E). In the presence of Ag+, the average lifetime of ox-CDs decreases from 4.58 to 2.66 ns. Interestingly, with the simultaneous addition of AChE and ATCh, the average lifetime of ox-CDs recovers to 4.61 ns, which is close to that at initial status (Fig. 2F and Table S2). This trend is similar to the Hg2+-based AChE activity detection strategy (Fig. S3F and Table S2). Herein, the mechanism of this metal ions quenching-based detection strategy is based on the competitive binding between ox-CDs/metal ions and sulfhydryl/metal ions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Molecular mechanism of interaction between CDs and metal ions
ce:section.ce:para:
Subsequently, the quenching behavior of Ag+ towards ox-CDs is studied as a model to study the molecular mechanism. With the addition of Ag+, the F 680 of CDs gradually decreases. Hence, the quenching process is studied by the well-known Stern-Volmer (SV) plots. It is apparent that SV plot is an upward curvature, which indicates the FL of ox-CDs is quenched by both static and dynamic quenching process ( Fig. 3A) [29]. As for this condition, the SV quenching constants (K SV) and the association constant (K S) can be determined by the following equation:
(1)
K
app
=
F 0
F
− 1
1
M
=
K
S V
+
K S
+
K S
K
S V
M

A plot of K app versus [M] yields a straight line with an intercept of K SV+K S and a slope of K SV K S. The individual values can be obtained from the solutions of the equation below:
(2)
K
S
2
−
K
S
I + S = 0

The K SV and K S of Ag+ towards the ox-CDs were determined to be 8.18 × 105 L/mol and 7.21 × 104 L/mol, respectively (Fig. 3B). Meanwhile, the absorption spectrum of ox-CDs in the presence of Ag+ changes obviously (Fig. 2E), demonstrating that the FL quenching may be caused by the static quenching [30]. The changes in the FL lifetime are the most reliable method to verify the quenching mechanism [31]. As listed in Fig. 2F and Table S2, the average lifetime of ox-CDs reduces from 4.91 to 2.66 ns in the presence of Ag+, indicating that the FL quenching of CDs by Ag+ is a dynamic quenching process, where Ag+ acts on the excited ox-CDs, thus endowing they return to the ground state without the emission of light. Hence, it is evident that both static and dynamic quenching are involved in the quenching of Ag+ towards ox-CDs.

The corresponding quenching constant (kq) of Ag+ towards ox-CDs is determined to be 1.67 × 1014 L/(mol·s) (based on kq=KSV/τ0, where τ0 is the lifetime of free ox-CDs), which is much larger than 107 L/(mol·s), generally indicative of either electron or energy transfer process [32]. Negligible overlap between FL spectra of ox-CDs and absorption band of Ag+ is observed (Fig. S4), which proves that the energy transfer from excited ox-CDs to Ag+ can be ignored. Moreover, the presence of TCh induces the recovery of FL intensity, which confirms the quenching of ox-CDs’ FL by Ag+ is reversible (Fig. S2). Meanwhile, the absorption spectrum of ox-CDs remains constant after continuous photoexcitation for 20 min in the presence of Ag+ (Fig. S5), exclude the possibility of photo chemical degradation of ox-CDs. Herein, we speculate that the quenching mechanism would be mainly derived from the photoinduced electron transfer (PET) process from excited ox-CDs to the Ag+.

Since the close distance between ox-CDs and Ag+ is required for apparent quenching, several structure alterations may occur in ox-CDs after coordination with Ag+. Jiao et al. attributed the quenching of Ag+ towards nitrogen-doped CDs (N-CDs) to the binding of Ag+ to the CO or CN functional groups of N-CDs [33]. Apparent shift is observed for several peaks attributed to νO-H/N-H, νC
O and νC-O, which indicates the strong interaction existed between surface functional groups of ox-CDs and Ag+ (Fig. S6). On the other hand, the Zeta potential of ox-CDs is determined to be −21.9 mV (Fig. S7), further indicating the possible electrostatic interaction between ox-CDs and Ag+.

The FL quenching of ox-CDs by Ag+ is the result of collisions and a complex formation process. Furthermore, the electrostatic and coordination interaction between Ag+ and surface functional groups of ox-CDs narrows their distance, resulting in the efficient quenching of FL of ox-CDs via electron transfer from excited CDs to Ag+.

Firstly, the FL performance of ox-CDs (indicated as F 680) is optimized. As shown in Fig. S8A, the F 680 versus concentration of ox-CDs exhibits a highly positive relation within the range between 0 and 10 μg/mL and decreases obviously when then the concentration further increases to 50 μg/mL. It is evident that F 680 gradually increases from pH = 3–7 and then decreases dramatically after the pH further increases to 12 (Fig. S8B). Herein, pH= 7 and 10 μg/mL ox-CDs are used in the following experiments.

Then several parameters related to the detection of AChE activity were optimized. As shown in Figs. S9A and 4A, the plots of the F 680 versus the concentration of ATCh for the ox-CDs-Ag+ system increase obviously within the range between 0 and 1 mmol/L and reach the platform when it further increases to 1.67 mmol/L. Similar results have been found for the ox-CDs-Hg2+ system while the range of obvious increasing located in the 0 and 0.1 mmol/L (Figs. S9B and S10A). Hence, 1 mmol/L and 0.1 mmol/L ATCh are applied for the ox-CDs-Ag+ and ox-CDs-Hg2+, respectively, in the following assay. Then the pH with fewer intervals (from 6.8 to 7.4) was optimized accordingly. As for the ox-CDs-Ag+ system, it is worth noting that though the F 680 increase is obviously within this range, the largest enhancement occurs at a pH value of 7.0 (Fig. 4B). This result is similar to ox-CDs-Hg2+ and the optimal pH was set as 7.1 (Fig. S10B). After the Ag+ is incubated with ox-CDs, the presence of hydrolyzed product (TCh) result in the dramatic increase of F 680 within 1 min and then finally reaches equilibrium (Fig. 4C). Similarity, only 2 min is required for recovery of the F 680 of the ox-CDs-Hg2+ system by TCh (Fig. 10C). Based on above results, the quenching of F 680 by metal ions were performed in 10 μg/mL ox-CDs, Tris-HAc buffer (pH=7 for Ag+ and pH=7.1 for Hg2+), 15 μmol/L Ag+ or 5 μmol/L Hg2+. Furthermore, the detection of AChE activity was conducted in 1 mmol/L ATCh (Ag+) and 0.1 mmol/L ATCh (Hg2+).

Subsequently, the AChE activity detection by the ox-CDs-Ag+ system under optimal conditions is verified accordingly. As shown in Fig. 4D, in the presence of AChE, the F 680 can be gradually enhanced in a concentration-dependent manner while the F 650 changes slightly. With the addition of AChE, the F 680/F 650 gradually increase and a great linearity relationship between F 680/F 650 and AChE activity is observed within the range between 0.5 and 10 mU/mL (Fig. 4E). Herein, the activity of AChE can be quantitatively determined by the following linear equation: F 680/F 650 = 0.268 × [AChE]+ 0.658, R2 = 0.996 (Fig. 4F). Moreover, the LOD is estimated to be 0.127 mU/mL. In addition, for the ox-CDs-Hg2+ system, the linear range and LOD are determined to be 0.05–1.6 mU/mL and 0.019 mU/mL (Fig. S10D–F, and Table S3). The LOD determined in this work is 1 order of magnitude lower than most of the existing fluorescent detection methods and commercial detection kits (Table S3). Although the linear range is much lower than AChE values of healthy people and at different risk levels [34], the content of AChE activity can be determined by diluting the biological to make the content of AChE into this range. It is very interesting that the linear range of the ox-CDs-Ag+ system is much broader than that of the ox-CDs-Hg2+ system, while the LOD of ox-CDs-Hg2+ system is much smaller. This result may be caused by the fact that the pKsp of HgS (51.80) is much higher than that of Ag2S (49.20) and the quenching efficiency of Hg2+ towards the F 680 of ox-CDs is much higher.

Then the influence of various interference species has been excluded accordingly. Firstly, the presence of other biomolecules, such as proteins, amino acids and enzymes, does not show any influence on the FL intensity of ox-CDs, which implies the superior physiological stability of ox-CDs under complex environment (Fig. S11). Moreover, the incubation of ATCh with the various enzyme for certation time do not induce any response on the F 680 of the aqueous solution of ox-CDs-Ag+. Furthermore, the mixing of other enzymes with AChE also contributes to the noticeable recovery of their FL intensity to initial status (Fig. S12A). Firstly, the influence of small molecules, especially the sulfhydryl-containing substances (Cys and GSH), on the FL properties of ox-CDs in the presence of metal ions has been investigated. As illustrated in Fig. S12B, the presence of AChE and ATCh induces the significant recovery of the FL intensity, while the other small molecular substance exhibits little influence on the ratio of F 680/F 650, except for the Cys and GSH. However, the presence of special thiols scavenger N-ethylmaleimide (NEM) [35] can efficiently eliminate the influences of GSH and Cys. Moreover, the presence of the above substances does not affect the recovery of the FL features of ox-CDs-Ag+ (Fig. S12C). In addition, the above enzyme and small molecular substances would not interfere with the detection of AChE activity by the ox-CDs-Hg2+ system (Fig. S12D–E). These results demonstrate that the feasibility of the ox-CDs and metal ions quenchants-based probe possesses high selectivity for monitoring the activity of AChE in the practical samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of AChE activity as a biomarker for OPs poisoning
ce:section.ce:para: These attractive features of the metal ions quenching strategy inspire us to detect the AChE activity as a biomarker for OPs intoxication. To ensure the reliability of the analytical probe, the recoveries are detected in aCSF spiked with different concentrations of AChE. For five standard concentrations of AChE, the recoveries of our proposed strategy in aCSF samples were located in the range of 95–105 %, with RSDs of 0.5–7.8 % (Table S4), demonstrating the reliability of the proposed analytical probe.

Subsequently, the feasibility of this analytical platform, with the ox-CDs-Ag+ system as a model, for the detection of plasma (peripheral system) and CSF (central nervous system) AChE has been confirmed, which can be served as a biomarker for OPs intoxication and treatment. In the presence of plasma and CSF samples from normal rats, obvious enhancement of F 680 and a slight increase at F 650 can be observed ( Fig. 5A and C), and related values of plasma and CSF AChE activity are determined to be 2713.1 ± 37.5 and 559.90 ± 23.0 mU/mL, respectively (Fig. 5B and D), which are close to previous values [36]. As for the rats exposed to dichlorvos, without any antidotal treatment, with the addition of plasma and CSF samples, the degrees of enhancement of F 680 are relatively smaller, and the related AChE activity in plasma and CSF is also much smaller. The activity of plasma and CSF AChE decline to 2383.0 ± 55.7 and 366.9 ± 3.4 mU/mL, respectively. The inactivation of AChE by dichlorvos may result in the accumulation of cholinergic receptors (ACh), thus inducing cholinergic syndrome in the central and peripheral nervous system and finally high lethality. The PAM, a clinically available antidote, was usually utilized to cure acute OPs intoxication. Then the changes in AChE activity after the detoxification treatment via pre-therapy (PAM-1) and post-therapy (PAM-2) with PAM were monitored. It is evident that the AChE can be reactivated by the injection with PAM (Fig. 5), indicating that the administration of PAM would protect AChE from the phosphorylation reaction of OPs. However, except for the plasma AChE of the PAM-2 group, the reactivation efficacy of AChE by PAM is limited, due to the activity of aging phosphorus AChE is hard to be recovered by PAM, so the acute OPs intoxication should be timely treated with appropriate therapy.

Recently, several works reported cerebrospinal AChE as a key biomarker for accessing OPs-induced neurotoxicity [21,37]. However, the inactivation of AChE in the peripheral blood can also be considered as an indicator of overall OPs-related intoxication [38]. As for our work, the fluctuation of plasma AChE activity in dichlorvos-poisoned and PAM-therapeutic rats can be monitored by the proposed ox-CDs-Ag+ platform. Clinically, the extracellular metabolic and pathological states of CNS can be reflected by the fluctuation of AChE in CSF, that is more potential for directly reflecting OPs intoxication status in CNS [36]. Interestingly, the levels of CSF AChE activity in rats can also be measured by the proposed ox-CDs-Ag+ nanoplatform. Overall, this ox-CDs-Ag+-based strategy is beneficial for detecting the AChE fluctuation in both peripheral blood and CSF in complex biological samples.

In this work, by the combination of intrinsic dual emissive ox-CDs (λ em=650 and 680 nm), metal ions (Ag+ or Hg2+) and ATCh, a novel ratiometric FL probe towards AChE activity have been fabricated. The sensing system is based on the F 680 that can be effectively quenched by the metal ions, while the other peak can be used as a self-calibration signal. Meanwhile, the simultaneous introduction of AChE and ATCh contributes to recovering the FL features of ox-CDs. Furthermore, the sensing nanoplatform can be employed for monitoring the fluctuation of plasma and CSF AChE activity in dichlorvos-poisoned and PAM-therapeutic rats, which is envisaged to be a key biomarker for better early diagnostics and prognostics of OPs-triggered neurotoxicity. This work provides a simple, rapid and highly sensitive analytical platform for the better understanding OPs poisoning related to the brain dysfunctions and provide protocols for the clinical therapy in a simple and reliable pattern.

Haimin Li: Investigation, Methodology, Writing – original draft. Yuqian Zhang：Investigation, Methodology. Xueting Luo: Investigation. Xueting Luo: Investigation. Yaqin Qu：Investigation, Methodology. Erjing Wang: review & editing. Bingbing Jiang: Writing – review & editing. Zhenpeng Qiu：Methodology, review & editing. Cao Li: Result interpretation. Ziqiang Xu: Conceptualization, Supervision, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We gratefully acknowledge financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (22073025, 21603067, 52073082).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.snb.2022.132590.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
Supplementary material
.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Induction of plasma acetylcholinesterase activity in mice challenged with organophosphorus poisons

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Ziqiang Xu received his BS degree from Hubei University in 2010. And then he obtained Ph.D. degree (2015) in chemistry under the supervision of Prof. Yi Liu from Wuhan University. Then he went back to Hubei University to work as a lecturer. He was promoted to Associate Professor in August 2018. Currently, his research focused on modulation of carbonized polymer dots, their biological effects and applications.
